regions.name: North Africa
regions.subtitle: The Maghreb Welcome
regions.description: Across the Maghreb—from Morocco's ancient medinas to Tunisia's coastal cities—tea is a ritual of hospitality. Poured from height into ornate glasses, mint tea symbolizes welcome, friendship, and the art of taking time.

culturalPairing.ceremony: The three glasses tradition — the first is gentle as life, the second strong as love, the third bitter as death.
culturalPairing.music: Gnawa rhythms and Andalusian classical melodies
culturalPairing.literature: Paul Bowles — The Sheltering Sky

culturalPairing.ceremony: Brewed in a small pot over coals, served in three rounds of increasing sweetness.
culturalPairing.music: Tinariwen — Desert blues and Tuareg guitar
culturalPairing.literature: Ibrahim al-Koni — The Bleeding of the Stone

teas.name: Moroccan Rosewater Tisane
teas.type: Herbal Infusion — Rose Petals, Orange Blossom, Verbena

culturalPairing.ceremony: Served after meals in the riads of Marrakech — a gentle, floral close to the evening.
culturalPairing.music: Andalusian oud — the melodies of the medina at dusk
culturalPairing.literature: Tahar Ben Jelloun — The Sacred Night

regions.name: East Africa
regions.subtitle: Highland Origins
regions.description: From Ethiopia's ancient forests to Kenya's sun-drenched highlands and Rwanda's terraced hills—East Africa is where tea began. Here, tea grows alongside coffee, creating unique traditions of morning and evening brews.

culturalPairing.ceremony: Brewed strong with spices in the Ethiopian highlands, often served with injera during morning gatherings.
culturalPairing.music: Ethiopian jazz — the fusion of traditional melodies with modern rhythms
culturalPairing.literature: Maaza Mengiste — The Shadow King

culturalPairing.ceremony: A rare purple-leaf variety rich in anthocyanins, brewed lighter to preserve its unique character.
culturalPairing.music: Benga rhythms — the upbeat sounds of Lake Victoria
culturalPairing.literature: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o — A Grain of Wheat

culturalPairing.ceremony: Known as 'Ishakwe' in Kinyarwanda, this vibrant red infusion is shared at community gatherings.
culturalPairing.music: Traditional drumming — the heartbeat of Rwandan celebrations
culturalPairing.literature: Scholastique Mukasonga — Our Lady of the Nile

regions.name: West Africa
regions.subtitle: Community Cups
regions.description: From Senegal's bustling streets to Ghana's coastal communities and Nigeria's vibrant markets—West African tea traditions blend indigenous herbs with colonial influences, creating unique communal ceremonies.

teas.name: Attaaya
teas.type: Green Tea with Mint and Sugar

culturalPairing.ceremony: Three rounds of increasingly sweet tea—'the first for your mother, the second for your father, the third for yourself.'
culturalPairing.music: Mbalax rhythms — the dance music of Dakar
culturalPairing.literature: Mariama Bâ — So Long a Letter

culturalPairing.ceremony: Sold on streets across Ghana, this bright red drink is the taste of everyday life.
culturalPairing.music: Highlife — the golden sounds of Ghanaian cities
culturalPairing.literature: Ayi Kwei Armah — The Beautyful Ones Are Not Yet Born

culturalPairing.ceremony: Chilled and served at celebrations across Nigeria, often with a hint of vanilla or clove.
culturalPairing.music: Afrobeat — the revolutionary sounds of Fela Kuti
culturalPairing.literature: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie — Half of a Yellow Sun

regions.name: Central Africa
regions.subtitle: Forest Infusions
regions.description: From Cameroon's mountain forests to Congo's river communities—Central African tea traditions draw deeply from the rainforest, incorporating native herbs, flowers, and healing plants passed down through generations.

culturalPairing.ceremony: A traditional medicinal herb used by forest communities, believed to bring clarity and strength.
culturalPairing.music: Makossa — the rhythmic sounds of Cameroonian nights
culturalPairing.literature: Mongo Beti — Mission to Kala

culturalPairing.ceremony: Known as 'the tree of life,' moringa leaves are brewed for their nutritional and healing properties.
culturalPairing.music: Soukous — the dance music of Kinshasa
culturalPairing.literature: Sony Labou Tansi — The Antipeople

culturalPairing.ceremony: Shared during community gatherings, this crimson infusion represents the vitality of the forest.
culturalPairing.music: Traditional harp music — the sounds of Gabonese forest ceremonies
culturalPairing.literature: Bessie Head — When Rain Clouds Gather

regions.name: Southern Africa
regions.subtitle: Bush Teas & Rooibos
regions.description: From South Africa's Cederberg mountains to Zimbabwe's highveld—Southern Africa offers unique teas born from the bush. Rooibos, honeybush, and buchu tell stories of indigenous knowledge and healing traditions.

culturalPairing.ceremony: Harvested by Khoisan communities for centuries, rooibos means 'red bush' in Afrikaans.
culturalPairing.music: Maskandi — the Zulu folk guitar tradition
culturalPairing.literature: Zakes Mda — Ways of Dying

culturalPairing.ceremony: Naturally sweet with notes of honey and apricot, this tea is fermented in the mountain sun.
culturalPairing.music: Mbira music — the traditional thumb piano of Zimbabwe
culturalPairing.literature: Yvonne Vera — Butterfly Burning

culturalPairing.ceremony: Used by San communities for generations, buchu is known for its distinctive minty-blackcurrant flavor.
culturalPairing.music: Traditional Tswana vocal harmonies
culturalPairing.literature: Unity Dow — Juggling Truths

regions.name: East Asia
regions.subtitle: The Way of Tea
regions.description: From Japan's meditative tea ceremonies to China's ancient Gongfu tradition and Taiwan's high-mountain oolongs, East Asian tea culture is a philosophy — a practice of presence, respect, and craft.

culturalPairing.ceremony: Once called 'the people's tea,' genmaicha was born from adding toasted rice to stretch precious tea leaves — warmth and humility in a cup.
culturalPairing.music: Koto — the 13-string plucked instrument of Japanese court music
culturalPairing.literature: Yasunari Kawabata — The Old Capital

culturalPairing.ceremony: Gongfu Cha — small clay pots, multiple short steeps, each pour revealing new layers of the leaf's character.
culturalPairing.music: Erhu — the two-string fiddle that sings like a human voice
culturalPairing.literature: Lin Yutang — The Importance of Living

culturalPairing.ceremony: Roasted over charcoal in porcelain pots, hojicha is the evening tea of Japan — warm, nutty, and naturally low in caffeine. A cup of calm.
culturalPairing.music: Shakuhachi flute — meditative bamboo tones
culturalPairing.literature: Kakuzo Okakura — The Book of Tea

regions.name: South America
regions.subtitle: The Circle of Yerba
regions.description: In Argentina, Uruguay, and southern Brazil, sharing mate from a single gourd is a tradition of equality and trust — a circle where everyone drinks from the same vessel.

culturalPairing.ceremony: The cebador prepares and serves. Saying 'gracias' means you're done. The gourd passes left.
culturalPairing.music: Chamame folk music of the Litoral region
culturalPairing.literature: Jorge Luis Borges — Ficciones

culturalPairing.ceremony: Shared among gauchos on the pampas, prepared fresh with a cuia and bomba.
culturalPairing.music: Milonga — the folk music of the southern plains
culturalPairing.literature: Érico Veríssimo — O Tempo e o Vento

teas.name: Mate Cocido de Hierbas
teas.type: Herbal Infusion — Mint, Lemon Verbena, Chamomile

culturalPairing.ceremony: An evening version of the mate circle — herbal, gentle, and perfect for those who want the warmth without the buzz.
culturalPairing.music: Zamba — the slow, swaying dance music of the Argentine north
culturalPairing.literature: Julio Cortázar — Hopscotch

regions.name: South Asia
regions.subtitle: Chai as Communion
regions.description: From the bustling chaiwallahs of Mumbai to the misty plantations of Darjeeling, tea in South Asia is woven into the rhythm of daily life — every street corner, every conversation.

culturalPairing.ceremony: Simmered slowly with cardamom, ginger, cinnamon, and cloves. Served in small clay cups — kulhads.
culturalPairing.music: Ravi Shankar — Sitar ragas at dusk
culturalPairing.literature: Arundhati Roy — The God of Small Things

culturalPairing.ceremony: Known as the 'Champagne of Teas,' best enjoyed without milk to appreciate its muscatel notes.
culturalPairing.music: Sarod melodies — the reflective tones of the hills
culturalPairing.literature: Ruskin Bond — A Town Called Dehra

teas.name: Kashmiri Kahwa
teas.type: Herbal Infusion — Saffron, Cinnamon, Cardamom, Almonds

culturalPairing.ceremony: Brewed in a traditional samovar, kahwa is the golden welcome of the Kashmir Valley — saffron threads floating in warmth.
culturalPairing.music: Santoor — the hammered dulcimer of the Kashmir Valley
culturalPairing.literature: Agha Shahid Ali — The Country Without a Post Office
